episode_meta.title: Vilas Dhar: Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human

use_cases.description: Using AI to source and validate story ideas and reporting leads
use_cases.outcome: Faster identification and validation of newsworthy stories

use_cases.description: Using AI to aggregate and analyze datasets gathered through investigative journalism, extracting interesting data patterns
use_cases.outcome: New analytic capabilities for datasets assembled through traditional reporting

use_cases.description: Streamlining bureaucratic processes like FOIA requests using AI
use_cases.outcome: Reduced administrative burden on investigative reporters

use_cases.description: Affirmative AI-powered fact-checking and verification of content authenticity
use_cases.outcome: Users can submit content and receive AI-driven assessment of whether it is real, testing the collateral and surrounding information ecosystem

use_cases.description: Repurposing high-quality investigative journalism for broader distribution or new formats
use_cases.outcome: Enables journalistic organizations to extend the reach and reuse of investigative work

use_cases.description: Using AI chatbots for personal information retrieval as a more efficient alternative to web search
use_cases.outcome: More efficient information lookup compared to traditional web search

use_cases.description: Using AI for collaborative family storytelling with children, co-creating fairy tales guided by human imagination
use_cases.outcome: Expanded imaginative experience while keeping creative control with humans

use_cases.description: Using AI as a Socratic interlocutor to challenge weaknesses in arguments and expand thinking, rather than as a content creator
use_cases.outcome: Forces the user to strengthen their own arguments and expand their imagination rather than relying on AI output

use_cases.description: Envisioning real-time AI fact-checker agents that sit alongside users as they consume information, flagging true vs. false content
use_cases.outcome: Real-time verification of information as it is consumed

use_cases.description: Using AI to automate less interesting tasks in the editorial process to free reporters for higher-value work
use_cases.outcome: Streamlined editorial process and broader AI literacy across the organization

key_claims.claim: Ethics is not an abstraction but should guide specific business decisions and technology implementations in newsrooms.
key_claims.speaker: Vilas Dhar

key_claims.claim: There is no way to be a media organization today without also being a technology organization; the idea that these are separate identities is a false dichotomy.
key_claims.speaker: Vilas Dhar

key_claims.claim: The time scale of AI transformation is fundamentally different from prior digital adoption: what was a 10-20 year transformation journey is now 18 months.
key_claims.speaker: Vilas Dhar

key_claims.claim: Reporters and journalists at the front line should have editorial control over how AI is used in their work, not just the data and analytics teams.
key_claims.speaker: Vilas Dhar
key_claims.evidence_type: normative argument based on historical precedent of tech-magnate media acquisitions

key_claims.claim: No company will innovate massive foundational AI changes for journalism unless it comes from within journalism itself, because journalism is not big enough to command its own market presence or be a profit driver.
key_claims.speaker: Vilas Dhar

key_claims.claim: People who don't have fundamental trust in media institutions are much more vulnerable to AI-enabled misinformation; AI is a symptom of the trust crisis, not its cause.
key_claims.speaker: Vilas Dhar

key_claims.claim: AI systems cannot understand vulnerability, pain, empathy, care, responsibility, or love; these remain uniquely human capacities.
key_claims.speaker: Vilas Dhar

key_claims.claim: We don't need bigger AI models; we need smaller, more targeted, more intelligent models that are more power-effective and use less water and energy.
key_claims.speaker: Vilas Dhar
key_claims.evidence_type: observation of innovators on the edges

key_claims.claim: Journalism has a unique opportunity to share what it learns and fails at with AI deployment, building a repository of complex decisions that applies across all sectors.
key_claims.speaker: Vilas Dhar

key_claims.claim: The biggest innovations from AI come when the technology is put into the hands of reporters and journalists, who then come up with the innovations themselves.
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

challenges.description: AI datasets used in newsrooms may unintentionally exclude voices or perspectives, affecting the breadth and diversity of stories told
Lack of industry consolidation around common standards, benchmarks, and frames for provenance tagging to distinguish real content from deepfakes
Crisis of public trust in media institutions makes audiences more vulnerable to AI-enabled misinformation
Organizations siloing AI and technology teams separately from editorial/journalism teams, preventing integrated ethical implementation
Enterprising journalists will use AI tools outside organizational environments if internal policies are too restrictive, creating data leakage and security vulnerabilities
Significant environmental cost of AI (energy, water) creates tension between AI's benefits for journalism and sustainability concerns
The build vs. buy dilemma: news organizations claiming they lack technical capacity to build tools, defaulting to vendor solutions that may not align with journalistic values
AI transformation is happening on an 18-month timescale vs. the 10-20 year cycles of previous digital adoption, forcing uncomfortable speed of adaptation
Journalistic success metrics are qualitative and values-driven rather than quantitative, making it harder to measure AI's impact compared to commercial sectors
Big tech companies are incentivized to build massive generalized AI models that maximize profits, not targeted models for journalism's specific needs
Potential creation of synthetic quotes or use of LLMs to generate content that makes stories feel stronger but violates foundational journalistic ethics

quotes.text: I don't think there is a way to be a media organization in this world today without also being a technology organization.
quotes.speaker: Vilas Dhar
quotes.topic: organizational identity in the AI era
quotes.pedagogical_value: Challenges the common excuse among news organizations that technology is not their domain; useful for framing discussions about digital transformation

quotes.text: An AI system cannot understand the vulnerability and pain of somebody who has just lost their house because of a bad discriminatory decision. Cannot understand what it feels like to be in the room as your son or daughter gets sentenced by an AI system for something they didn't do. Can't understand empathy or care, responsibility, or love. These are things that are uniquely human in this world.
quotes.speaker: Vilas Dhar
quotes.topic: human agency and the limits of AI
quotes.pedagogical_value: Powerful articulation of what remains irreplaceably human in journalism; directly addresses student/practitioner anxiety about AI displacement

quotes.text: I am very rarely optimistic about AI. But I am very optimistic about well-intentioned, values-driven people that are making decisions about how we use AI.
quotes.speaker: Vilas Dhar
quotes.pedagogical_value: Important reframing that shifts the locus of hope from technology to human decision-making; useful for teaching responsible AI adoption

quotes.text: Who's incentivized to build AI models exclusively for journalism? A sector that maybe is not big enough to command its own market presence that isn't going to be that profit driver. Well, this is the challenge we've struggled with for the last decade is there's not going to be some set of companies that are going to say we're going to innovate massive foundational changes in AI for journalism unless it comes from within journalism itself.
quotes.speaker: Vilas Dhar
quotes.pedagogical_value: Frames the structural market failure for journalism-specific AI and makes the case for journalism building its own tools; central to the build vs. buy debate

quotes.text: I don't want you to create anything, and I don't want you to introduce anything new. I just want you to ask me a lot of questions. I want you to ask me questions that force me to tangle with the weakness of my argument or what I have been too limited in my imagination around.
quotes.speaker: Vilas Dhar
quotes.topic: using AI as a Socratic interlocutor
quotes.pedagogical_value: Concrete, actionable example of reconceptualizing the human-AI relationship where AI serves as intellectual challenger rather than content generator; directly applicable in classroom and newsroom settings

quotes.text: We should be asking, how do we transform our social practices so that we can leverage the ways we use these tools to minimize the queries we need, but get much better outcomes from them?
quotes.speaker: Vilas Dhar
quotes.pedagogical_value: Reframes the environmental cost debate from a per-query technical problem to a behavioral and social practice question; useful for teaching sustainable AI use

recommendations.advice: Start with responsible data practices: vet the provenance of data, understand who is represented in datasets, and identify how data collection may unintentionally exclude voices or perspectives before deploying AI tools.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

recommendations.advice: Set bright-line rules about what AI will never be used for (e.g., creation of synthetic quotes, unattributed sources, LLM-generated content to artificially strengthen stories) before focusing on what AI can do.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

recommendations.advice: Ensure transparency by disclosing AI use and potentially partnering every story with an explanation of how AI was used in that particular piece of reporting.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

recommendations.advice: Ensure that reporters, editors, data teams, and executives are all equally AI-literate rather than siloing AI knowledge in a technology team.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

recommendations.advice: Shift AI implementation power to front-line reporters and journalists who understand the medium, rather than letting technology teams or executives drive adoption top-down.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

recommendations.advice: Create intentional sandbox environments where journalists can experiment with AI tools without creating data leakage or security vulnerabilities, rather than restrictive policies that push experimentation outside organizational controls.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

recommendations.advice: Move from consuming commercial AI tools to building journalism-specific tools in-house or through collaboratives; this paradigm shift changes the economics and quality of reporting.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

recommendations.advice: Reduce AI environmental footprint by consolidating multiple queries into fewer, better-crafted ones and by building collaboratives where organizations share data, models, and resources.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

recommendations.advice: Use AI as a Socratic interlocutor: instruct AI to only ask questions that challenge your arguments and expand your thinking, rather than using it to generate content.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

recommendations.advice: Undertake an organizational reckoning to ask 'who do we aspire to be in an AI-enabled world?' rather than simply choosing between vendor A and vendor B.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

recommendations.advice: Turn AI deployment experiments into behind-the-scenes reporting: share with your audience how an article was assembled, where AI was considered and where it was rejected, building trust and creating a public repository of decision-making that benefits all sectors.
recommendations.speaker: Vilas Dhar

external_references: LinkedIn Learning course on ethics in generative AI
Three-part ethical AI framework (Responsible Data, Clear Boundaries, Transparency)
